summary: Yegge argues that programmer maturity can be measured by their 'compression tolerance' - the ability to read and write dense code without excessive comments or metadata. He compares junior programmers' verbose commenting style to a 2-year-old's temporal narratives, both being attempts to make sense of complexity through storytelling. The post extends this metaphor to static typing, arguing that over-reliance on type systems and metadata modeling is another form of n00b behavior. He concludes that experienced programmers should resist the urge to model everything and instead focus on getting code written and making forward progress.
key_points: Programmer maturity correlates with tolerance for code compression - n00bs need verbose comments while veterans prefer dense, self-documenting code
Static types are just metadata/comments targeted at programmers and compilers, and can be overused just like comments
Java's success partly comes from enabling 'metadata addiction' - letting programmers spend time modeling instead of shipping
The more 'sound' a type system tries to be, the more often it's 'wrong' when it can't express what you actually need
When in doubt, don't model it - excessive metadata has real costs in maintenance, flexibility, and development speed
money_quotes: If you're a n00b, you'll look at experienced code and say it's impenetrable, undisciplined crap written by someone who never learned the essentials of modern software engineering.

Static types tell a story about the computation, presumably to help both reader groups understand the intent of the program. But the static types can be thrown away at runtime, because in the end they're just stylized comments.
Perl, Python and Ruby fail to attract many Java and C++ programmers because, well, they force you to get stuff done.
Haskell, OCaml and their ilk are part of a 45-year-old static-typing movement within academia to try to force people to model everything. Programmers hate that.
Java has been overrun by metadata-addicted n00bs.
When in doubt, don't model it. Just get the code written, make forward progress.
A sufficiently large model can outweigh its own benefits. Even an IDE can't make things go faster when you have ten thousand classes in your system.
tone: provocative, irreverent, and deliberately contrarian with characteristic Yegge humor and self-deprecation
key_insight: Excessive commenting, static typing, and metadata modeling are all manifestations of the same insecurity - the n00b's need to tell themselves stories about code rather than just writing it.
